items.mistake: Assuming any interesting set of vectors is automatically a subspace.
items.fix: Always check the three subspace criteria: contains the zero vector, closed under addition, and closed under scalar multiplication.
items.why: Lines that do not pass through the origin, spheres and half-spaces are common counterexamples; using subspace tools on them breaks proofs and algorithms.
items.example: The set of points `(x, y)` with `y = 1` in R^2 is not a subspace: it does not contain the zero vector and is not closed under scalar multiplication.

items.mistake: Confusing spanning sets with bases and ignoring linear dependence.
items.fix: When you choose a set of vectors to serve as a basis, verify both that they span the space and that no vector is a linear combination of the others.
items.why: Redundant vectors increase dimension counts incorrectly, make coordinate representations non-unique and can cause numerical instability in solvers.
items.example: In R^2, the three vectors `(1, 0)`, `(0, 1)` and `(1, 1)` span the space but do not form a basis because `(1, 1)` is a linear combination of the first two.

items.mistake: Treating coordinates as intrinsic properties rather than basis-dependent.
items.fix: Always tie coordinates to a specific basis and state which basis you are using when interpreting or comparing coordinate vectors.
items.why: The same geometric vector can have very different coordinate representations in different bases, which can mislead interpretation or debugging.
items.example: The vector pointing along the diagonal in R^2 has coordinates `(1, 1)` in the standard basis, but `(sqrt(2), 0)` in an orthonormal basis aligned with the diagonal and anti-diagonal directions.
